norepinefrina.clinicalNotes.0: Catecholamine with potent vasoconstrictor action (alpha-1) and positive inotropic effect (beta-1).
norepinefrina.clinicalNotes.1: First-choice drug in septic shock (Surviving Sepsis 2021).
norepinefrina.clinicalNotes.3: Doses above 0.5 mcg/kg/min indicate refractory shock — consider adding vasopressin.
norepinefrina.clinicalNotes.4: Preferably administer via central venous access.
vasopressina.clinicalNotes.0: Antidiuretic hormone with vasoconstrictor action (V1 receptors).
vasopressina.clinicalNotes.1: Second-line in septic shock, combined with norepinephrine (Surviving Sepsis 2021).
vasopressina.clinicalNotes.3: Do not titrate by weight — fixed dose.
vasopressina.clinicalNotes.4: Doses above 0.04 U/min associated with mesenteric and digital ischemia.
dobutamina.clinicalNotes.0: Synthetic catecholamine with predominant positive inotropic action (beta-1).
dobutamina.clinicalNotes.1: Drug of choice in cardiogenic shock and acute heart failure.
dobutamina.clinicalNotes.3: Positive chronotropic effect — monitor for tachycardia.
dobutamina.clinicalNotes.4: May cause hypotension via vasodilation (beta-2) at low doses.
dopamina.clinicalNotes.0: Endogenous catecholamine with dose-dependent effects: dopaminergic, beta, and alpha.
dopamina.clinicalNotes.2: Dose 3-10 mcg/kg/min: beta-1 effect (inotropic and chronotropic).
dopamina.clinicalNotes.4: Higher incidence of arrhythmias compared to norepinephrine (SOAP II Trial).
epinefrina.clinicalNotes.0: Endogenous catecholamine with alpha and beta-adrenergic action.
epinefrina.clinicalNotes.1: Indicated in anaphylactic shock and as second-line in septic shock.
epinefrina.clinicalNotes.3: Potent chronotropic effect — risk of tachyarrhythmias.
epinefrina.clinicalNotes.4: Can be used as alternative to dobutamine in cardiogenic shock.
fentanila.clinicalNotes.0: Potent synthetic opioid with rapid onset of action.
fentanila.clinicalNotes.1: Analgesia and sedation in mechanically ventilated patients (PADIS 2018).
fentanila.clinicalNotes.2: Usual continuous infusion dose: 0.5 to 3 mcg/kg/h.
fentanila.clinicalNotes.3: Risk of chest wall rigidity with rapid bolus.
fentanila.clinicalNotes.4: Monitor for respiratory depression and hypotension.
midazolam.clinicalNotes.0: Short-acting benzodiazepine with sedative, anxiolytic, and amnestic properties.
midazolam.clinicalNotes.1: ICU sedation for mechanically ventilated patients.
midazolam.clinicalNotes.3: Accumulation with prolonged infusions — prefer propofol or dexmedetomidine (PADIS 2018).
propofol.clinicalNotes.3: Watch for propofol infusion syndrome (PRIS) at doses > 5 mg/kg/h for > 48h.
propofol.clinicalNotes.4: Contains lipids — consider caloric intake. Change syringe every 12h.
cetamina.clinicalNotes.0: Dissociative anesthetic with analgesic and bronchodilator properties.
cetamina.clinicalNotes.1: Useful as adjunct for ICU sedation and analgesia.
cetamina.clinicalNotes.4: May cause sialorrhea, hallucinations, and ICP elevation at high doses.
dexmedetomidina.clinicalNotes.0: Central alpha-2 agonist with sedative and analgesic properties.
dexmedetomidina.clinicalNotes.1: Cooperative sedation — patient arousable to stimulation (PADIS 2018).
dexmedetomidina.clinicalNotes.3: Lower risk of delirium compared to benzodiazepines.
dexmedetomidina.clinicalNotes.4: May cause bradycardia and hypotension. Do NOT dilute in D5W.
morfina.clinicalNotes.0: Natural opioid with potent analgesic action.
morfina.clinicalNotes.1: Usual continuous infusion dose: 1 to 5 mg/h.
morfina.clinicalNotes.2: Active metabolite (M6G) accumulates in renal failure.
morfina.clinicalNotes.3: May cause histamine release — avoid in bronchospasm.
morfina.clinicalNotes.4: Monitor sedation, respiratory depression, and bowel motility.
rocuronio.clinicalNotes.0: Aminosteroid neuromuscular blocking agent with intermediate duration.
rocuronio.clinicalNotes.1: Indicated for neuromuscular paralysis in mechanically ventilated patients.
rocuronio.clinicalNotes.3: ALWAYS ensure adequate sedation and analgesia — paralyzed patients feel pain.
rocuronio.clinicalNotes.4: Monitor with TOF (Train of Four) every 4-6h. Target: TOF 1-2/4.
atracurio.clinicalNotes.0: Benzylisoquinolinium neuromuscular blocking agent with intermediate duration.
atracurio.clinicalNotes.1: Hofmann elimination — safe in renal and hepatic failure.
atracurio.clinicalNotes.3: ALWAYS ensure adequate sedation and analgesia.
atracurio.clinicalNotes.4: May cause histamine release — caution in asthmatic patients.
cisatracurio.clinicalNotes.0: Cis-cis isomer of atracurium, 3x more potent.
cisatracurio.clinicalNotes.1: Hofmann elimination — ideal for hepatic/renal failure.
cisatracurio.clinicalNotes.4: Monitor with TOF. Drug of choice for prolonged NMB in ICU (ACURASYS Trial).
nitroglicerina.clinicalNotes.0: Predominantly venous vasodilator (low doses) and arterial (high doses).
nitroglicerina.clinicalNotes.1: Indicated in acute coronary syndrome and hypertensive emergency.
nitroglicerina.clinicalNotes.4: Use special tubing (non-PVC adsorbing) at high concentrations.
nitroprussiato.clinicalNotes.0: Direct arterial and venous vasodilator (nitric oxide).
nitroprussiato.clinicalNotes.1: Drug of choice in hypertensive emergency with encephalopathy.
nitroprussiato.clinicalNotes.3: Risk of cyanide toxicity with prolonged use (> 48h) or doses > 2 mcg/kg/min.
nitroprussiato.clinicalNotes.4: PROTECT FROM LIGHT — use light-protective tubing.
